text: Welcome. Today we will be discussing a laparoscopic labs procedure in a patient with situs inversus and a pre-duodenal portal vein.

Situs inversus is a rare congenital anomaly associated with one in four thousand to one in ten thousand live births. It presents where the major visceral organs are presented as a mirror image of their normal position, known as situs solidus.

It may present with dextrocardia or other congenital anomalies such as malotation or a pre-duodenal portal vein. Malrotation is associated with sitrus inversus. It occurs much higher incidence with sitrus inversus at approximately one in five hundred births.

Malrotation is a failure of the stage two of intestinal development between weeks ten and eleven. During this the sequel colonic loop and the duodenal jejunal loop pass in a counterclockwise rotation around the superior mesenteric artery.

Many patients with malrotation are asymptomatic. If they are asymptomatic, they may present with abdominal discomfort, intermittent or chronic obstruction, or symptoms associated with gastroesophageal reflux, with bilious or non-bilious emesis.

There is controversy surrounding operative intervention with situs inversus and malrotation. Some say to correct all forms of malrotation. Some only correct symptomatic malrotation, such as reflux symptoms. The literature advises against performing this in fundoplication if there is evidence of malrotation seen on operative intervention.

Diagnosis of malrotation is obtained through an upper GI study. A pre-duodenal portal vein was first described in the nineteen twenties. Fifty percent of patients with pre-duodenal portal veins present with high intestinal obstruction. The other fifty percent are asymptomatic. It is a rare anomaly that is described in only eighty-two patients in the literature and can be associated with other anomalies such as malrotation and situs inversus.

our story begins with a twenty-month-old male who presented with persistent reflux symptoms his initial presentation at eleven months of age were with those of reflux and non bilious emesis he had an upper gi performed at this time confirming sitius inversus abdominis without evidence of midgut volvulus or obstruction but evidence of mount rotation

He was managed non-operatively for approximately nine months. He returned at eighteen months of age, again with emesis. An upper GI at that time showed no evidence of obstruction. He was sent home and again presented two months later, but at this time with failure to thrive and a five kilogram weight loss.
Due to failure to thrive, the patient's family was permitted for a laparoscopic LADS procedure, possible fundoplication.
The abdominal x-ray reveals a right-sided stomach, a midline liver, and normal thoracic situs, indicating anatomical variations consistent with situs inversus.

Here we review the upper GI studies with no evidence of obstruction. They did show heterotaxy with a right-sided stomach, normal thoracic situs, intestinal malrotation with an abnormal duodenal sweep, and a left upper quadrant cecum. Here right-sided stomach.

The imaging shows a right-sided stomach with the duodenum crossing the midline. There is an abnormal duodenal sweep not crossing the midline a second time. The left upper quadrant displays bowel congestion, with small bowel congestion visible in the left upper quadrant and pelvic region. The small bowel is seen approaching the cecum, with some dilated loops present.
The small bowel is seen approaching the cecum in the left upper quadrant, while the large bowel is observed crossing the midline.

The LADS procedure was developed to correct the fundamental abnormalities associated with malrotation by Dr. William LADS. The steps include mobilization of the right colon, a counterclockwise or a derotation of the midgut volvulus if it is present, division of LADS bands, mobilization of the duodenum, division of adhesions to broaden the mesenteric base, and typically appendectomy.

Performance of laparoscopic versus open LADS procedure is supported by the literature. Here we present our video of a laparoscopic LADS procedure in this patient with cytosine versus in a pre-tubinal portal vein.
Upon initial entrance into the abdomen, the surgeon observes the right-sided spleen, midline liver, right-sided stomach, and gallbladder.
The surgeon observes extensive peritoneal attachments to surrounding structures, with Ladd's bands visible between the loops of small bowel and at the base of the gallbladder.

The video shows engorged and dilated large vessels on the right side of the screen. Enlarged loops of small bowel are visible in the left upper quadrant, with some small bowel diving posterior and inferior to the mesentery.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to perform extensive adhesiolysis, carefully reducing peritoneal attachments to mobilize the duodenum and small bowel, and to explore the base of the liver.

The surgeon examines the inferior wall of the gallbladder, revealing a pre-duodenal portal vein in the middle of the screen. The duodenum is visible coursing posterior to the vein and extending into the left upper quadrant.
The surgical view shows a right-sided stomach and spleen, a midline liver, and the gallbladder positioned on the right side of the screen. The anatomy is noted to be abnormal.

The literature shows a decreased time to starting feeds and decreased time to full feeding associated with laparoscopic surgery, shows a decreased amount of wound infections, a decreased post-operative length of stay, decreased rates of post-operative small bowel obstruction, and incidentally no increase in the recurrence of alveolus.

Not shown in this video due to technical difficulties. would be the portion of the LADS procedure associated with mobilization of the right colon. In this patient, he had a left-sided colon. The colon did have attachments to the small bowel that are typical of a LADS procedure.

These were taken down and reduced, but again, not shown here. Pertinent anatomy again, a midline liver, a right-sided spleen, a right-sided stomach with the pylorus coursing posterior to the portal vein, and the duodenum in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon dissects and removes peritoneal attachments and Ladd's bands, revealing the cause of the patient's intermittent obstruction due to small bowel kinking. Engorged vessels are visible, likely related to the kinking and pre-duodenal portal vein. As the dissection progresses, the duodenum and small bowel are straightened into a non-obstructive anatomical position, with the portal vein becoming more apparent.
Following the reduction of peritoneal attachments, the video shows extensive vascular congestion in the duodenum, likely caused by previous kinking before adhesiolysis.

The still frame shows the portal vein on the left, engorged vessels on the right, and the liver at the top of the screen. The small bowel is being manipulated down towards the cecum, revealing LADS bands attaching the small bowel to the cecum, which are reduced to further expose the appendix for the final portion of the procedure.
The appendix is identified in the right lower quadrant, appearing non-inflamed.
The appendix is extracorporealized and suture ligation is performed. The surgeon uses electrocautery to seal the opening of the appendix, concluding the procedure.

Long-term complications from LADS procedures approach ten percent. Typically, small bowel obstruction, recurrent bowel rotation, and mid-gut bogulus are the most common. Of importance in our patient, he started on a diet on post-op day one. He was advanced to a regular diet on post-op day two and discharged on his fourth hospital day. He had no symptoms of reflux or emesis, and he is now gaining weight.
